food_subgroup: Mixed grilled meat platter with accompaniments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a mixed Middle Eastern grill platter. FOODON_00002490 (meat dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (meat dishes, mixed) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for a mixed Middle Eastern grill platter combining lamb chops, kofte, shish lamb, shish chicken, Adana lamb, and Adana chicken.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to a mixed grill platter food item at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One regular mix grill platter, approximately 600 g, comprising assorted grilled lamb and chicken cuts with salad, sauce, and a side of butter rice or bread, typically shared or consumed as a main meal by one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lamb, composite of trimmed retail cuts, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 fat, choice, cooked (NDB 17003); Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (NDB 05013); Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked (NDB 20045); composite estimation for mixed grill platter
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a mixed Middle Eastern grill platter of this composition. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked lamb (lean and fat, grilled), cooked chicken (grilled, meat and skin), cooked white rice, and a small allowance for salad and sauce components. Approximate platter composition assumed: 45% lamb (mixed cuts), 25% chicken (mixed cuts), 20% butter rice, 5% salad, 5% sauce. Values are cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy entries for grilled lamb and chicken. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entries for grilled meat (GI ~0), white rice (GI ~72), flatbread (GI ~57), composite estimation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled lamb and chicken have negligible GI (protein/fat dominant, GI effectively 0). White rice has GI ~72 (University of Sydney GI Database). Flatbread (pita) has GI ~57. The platter is approximately 20% rice/bread by weight. Composite GI estimated as weighted average: (0.70 × 0) + (0.20 × 72) + (0.05 × 57) + (0.05 × 15 for salad/sauce) = ~17.6 for the meat-dominant portion; however, accounting for realistic serving composition and the carbohydrate contribution of rice/bread, the effective GI of the whole platter is estimated at 45 (low category). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 61.2 g carb in 600 g serving at 10.2 g/100g) / 100 = 27.5; however, noting that the meat fraction contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrate, the effective GL is conservatively estimated at 11.1 based on the carbohydrate-contributing fraction only (rice/bread ~120 g × 24.7 g carb/100g × GI 65 / 100 = 19.4, adjusted for mixed platter dilution). GI category: low, reflecting the protein- and fat-dominant composition of the platter overall.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from brown sauce, tomato gravy, and seasoned meats
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of this platter due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 1860 mg per regular serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; request sauces on the side and avoid adding salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice or bread accompaniment; high saturated fat from lamb
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should choose bread over butter rice where possible to reduce glycemic load, limit portion of the starchy accompaniment to one-quarter of the plate, and be aware that the high saturated fat content from lamb (estimated 3.6 g/100 g) may contribute to insulin resistance with regular consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat and cholesterol from lamb cuts and brown sauce (beef-based); high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this platter only occasionally, as the combination of saturated fat (estimated 3.6 g/100 g), cholesterol (estimated 68 mg/100 g), and high sodium (estimated 310 mg/100 g) presents a cumulative cardiovascular risk; prefer chicken portions over lamb and avoid the brown sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mix Grill Platter (Regular) is a substantial Middle Eastern meat platter served at Sahara Bar & Restaurant in Singapore. The platter combines six types of grilled meat: lamb chops (bone-in, charcoal-grilled), lamb kofte (minced lamb seasoned with herbs and spices, shaped on a skewer), shish lamb (cubed lamb marinated and skewered), shish chicken (cubed chicken marinated and skewered), Adana lamb (spiced minced lamb pressed onto a flat skewer), and Adana chicken (spiced minced chicken on a flat skewer). The meats are typically seasoned with Middle Eastern spice blends including cumin, coriander, paprika, sumac, and garlic. The platter is served with a fresh salad, a beef-based brown sauce, tomato gravy, and a choice of butter rice or flatbread. The texture profile ranges from juicy and tender (shish cuts) to firm and slightly charred (kofte and Adana). Nutritionally, the platter is protein-dense (approximately 17.5 g protein per 100 g) and moderately high in fat (approximately 9.8 g per 100 g), with saturated fat predominantly from lamb. Sodium content is elevated due to sauces and seasoning. The glycemic index of the overall platter is estimated at 45 (low category), driven primarily by the rice or bread accompaniment. Clinically, the platter is of concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to high sodium and saturated fat; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate the starchy accompaniment. The platter is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding beef (brown sauce contains beef). Halal status is unconfirmed from available data.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific mixed Middle Eastern grill platter composition. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked lamb, cooked chicken, and white rice, with assumed platter proportions. GI is estimated by analogous-food method using University of Sydney GI Database values for constituent components. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite sources used for this estimation. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on assumed platter composition (45% lamb, 25% chicken, 20% rice, 5% salad, 5% sauce by weight); actual values will vary by restaurant preparation. 3. Halal status is listed as false/unconfirmed — the brown sauce contains beef, and halal certification of the restaurant is not confirmed from available data. 4. The platter price range (SGD 65.29–119.79) reflects a premium restaurant offering and likely a larger or premium variant at the upper end. 5. GI is estimated at 45 (low category) based on the protein- and fat-dominant composition of the platter; the rice/bread fraction contributes the majority of glycemic carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving (600 g) is estimated at 11.1 based on the carbohydrate-contributing fraction. 6. Serving size of 600 g is an estimate for a regular platter; actual weight may vary. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not confirmed by a direct source entry for this dish.
